summary: McKenzie provides a comprehensive guide to software registration and copy protection for micro-ISVs, arguing that registration systems should be thought of as salesmen rather than security measures. He categorizes users into four types — from perfectly honest to dedicated pirates — and explains that protection schemes should target the middle groups while accepting that determined pirates are unbeatable. The post covers crack types (leaked keys, keygens, patches, cracked executables), defensive strategies (user-specific data, code obfuscation, frequent binary changes), and key generation approaches from public-key crypto to simple modular arithmetic. His core message is that developers should invest proportionally in protection, aiming to deter casual piracy rather than achieve impossible perfection.
key_points: Registration systems are marketing tools (salesmen), not security mechanisms — overly aggressive DRM harms paying customers more than it deters pirates
Users fall into four groups: perfectly honest, mostly honest, opportunistic pirates, and dedicated pirates — protection schemes primarily convert the 'mostly honest' group into paying customers
Every protection scheme will eventually be cracked; the goal is to keep circumvention obscure enough that casual users won't find or use cracks
Developers should force attackers toward less convenient crack types (from leaked keys up to cracked executables) through user-specific data, obfuscation, and frequent binary changes
The emotional violation developers feel about piracy leads to irrational over-investment in protection that punishes legitimate customers
money_quotes: You will have an easier time conceptualizing your registration scheme if you think of it as primarily a marketing, rather than technical, measure. Its your salesman that encourages folks to pay you money.
Salesmen do not typically kick their prospective customers where the sun doesn't shine, and many registration schemes do.

Someone who downloads your software and cracks it hasn't cost you any more money than someone who picks your door and walks around your apartment for 20 minutes without touching anything, yet the feeling that your rights have been violated is the same.
No protection scheme will survive indefinite contact with the adversary.
Your goal in instituting a protection scheme is not to achieve 0 utilization of your software by the Jolly Rogers of the world.

Defeating the cracker does not make you an appreciable amount of money. You just need to defeat the casual user in most instances, and the casual user does not have access to a debugger nor know how to use one.
Consider carefully how much pain you will authorize him to inflict.
key_insight: Software copy protection is a marketing tool that should convert mostly-honest users into paying customers, not a security system that tries — and inevitably fails — to stop determined pirates.
